title: The Bag of Words Risk
concept: Because self-attention compares all words in parallel, it ignores word order (treating 'cat eats fish' and 'fish eats cat' identically).

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between the sentences 'Dog bites man' and 'Man bites dog'?

options.text: The font size of the letters

exercises.explain: In human language, changing word order changes the meaning. 'No school today' is great news, but 'School no today' is confusing! AI needs to learn this order too.

cards.title: Order Matters!
cards.body: Have you ever sent a text where changing the order of words completely changed your message? In AI, if we aren't careful, computers can easily mix up your words like a salad!

cards.title: The Bag of Words Risk
cards.body: By default, self-attention processes all words in a sentence at the exact same time. Because there is no step-by-step reading, the model treats the sentence as an unordered 'Bag of Words', completely ignoring the sequence of the words.

cards.title: The Word Soup
cards.body: Imagine writing a letter, cutting up all the words, and tossing them into a bag. If you pull them out, you might get "dog bites man" or "man bites dog"—you've lost the order. That's exactly how raw self-attention sees a sentence!

cards.title: Who Cooked Whom?
cards.body: Without order, the sentences "The chef cooked the bear" and "The bear cooked the chef" look exactly the same to the model. It cannot tell who is cooking and who is being cooked!

cards.title: The Smart AI Myth
cards.body: You might think that because AI is smart, it automatically understands that words on the left come before words on the right. In reality, self-attention has no built-in sense of direction or sequence at all!

cards.title: Visualizing the Bag of Words
cards.body: See how losing the sequence of words changes how a sentence is understood.

left.text: 'Cat eats fish' (Clear meaning of who is eating whom)

right.text: {cat, eats, fish} (Just a pile of words, order is lost)

exercises.prompt: Why does raw self-attention ignore the order of words in a sentence?

options.text: It processes all words in parallel at the same time
It reads words one-by-one from right to left
It only looks at the first and last words of a sentence
It translates every word into a different language first

exercises.explain: Because self-attention processes all words simultaneously (in parallel), it has no inherent way to know which word came first, second, or last.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Without position clues, self-attention treats 'dog chases cat' and 'cat chases dog' as the exact same sentence.
exercises.explain: Since raw self-attention treats sentences as unordered bags of words, 'A eats B' and 'B eats A' are seen as identical.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe the main limitation of raw self-attention.
exercises.sentence: Without extra help, self-attention completely ignores word ________.
exercises.explain: Because self-attention looks at all words at the same time, it loses track of their order unless we add extra information to help it.

exercises.prompt: Which of these models is showing the Bag of Words error?

options.text: AI Model A says: 'Cats chase mice' means the cats are doing the chasing.
AI Model B says: 'Cats chase mice' and 'Mice chase cats' mean the exact same thing.
AI Model C says: The word 'chase' is an action verb in this sentence.

exercises.explain: AI Model B makes the classic 'Bag of Words' mistake by treating two sentences with the exact same words in different orders as having the identical meaning.

exercises.prompt: If a raw self-attention network (with no position information) processes the phrases 'Not good, bad' and 'Bad, not good', what happens?

options.text: It will recognize the negation 'not' is closer to 'good'
It will treat them as completely identical because word order is ignored
It will focus only on the punctuation marks like commas
It will automatically sort the words alphabetically first

exercises.explain: Without positional embeddings, self-attention processes words in parallel without any sense of layout. Therefore, 'Not good, bad' and 'Bad, not good' look identical to the mathematical operations.
